![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

# SLOTH

Markdown to slide creator and presenter, in a terminal interface.

## Features

-   Turn markdown into beautiful slide presentations
-   Share slides with your audience without sharing your screen
-   Run code directly in your presentation (currently Node; Rust and Python coming soon)
-   Host actions (like change slide, run code) are synchronously triggered across all users
-   Send surveys to users and collect responses directly without cloud infrastructure
-   Easily share downloads, including your presentation deck
-   Convert your presentation into a PDF or PPT file 
-   Choose between HTML or Terminal style for your audience
-   Pick from over 500 themes
-   Sync your phone with your presentation, to use it as a clicker
-   Create wikilinks to other decks, for multiperson presentations

**

## Running it

**

**

You can use it now at sloth.browserpod.io. But you can also build it. 

SLOTH is a [Vite](https://vitejs.dev/) app that boots a BrowserPod instance, ships SLOTH's source into the Pod, and runs it.

```
npm install
npm run dev
```

Open the URL Vite prints (default [http://localhost:5173](http://localhost:5173/)). You need a BrowserPod API key in `.env` as `VITE_BP_APIKEY` (get one at [https://console.browserpod.io](https://console.browserpod.io/)).

Other scripts:

-   `npm run build` / `npm run preview` — production build / preview
-   `npm run pod-modules` — rebuild the bundled Node dependency tree shipped into the Pod (runs automatically before dev/build)
-   `node scripts/build-gogh-themes.mjs` — regenerate the Gogh themes from `Gogh-master/themes/` into `lib/gogh-themes.js`

To prioritise speed, I've not installed the dependencies in the Pod at runtime. Instead writing into the Pod's filesystem before it starts, so there's no npm install step and no dependency on a registry.

But you can pick either approach if you choose to do your own build.

  

Pricing & Licensing

-   SLOTH is free to use without limits
-   SLOTH is open source, but requires a BrowserPod API key to create sharable links without a server-side infrastructure



**

**Why SLOTH?** 

It stands for **S**hare, **L**ovingly **O**ver-featured **T**erminal-**H**osted slide decks.

SLOTH is a terminal markdown presentation tool that runs entirely in the browser. It started as the tiny `---`\-split presenter [tslide](https://github.com/dominictarr/tslide) and grew to fold in the strengths of a half-dozen other terminal markdown tools, then was wrapped in [BrowserPod](https://browserpod.io) so the whole thing, a real Node.js process and terminal, runs client-side with nothing installed and no data leaving the page.

You write decks as plain markdown, present them in a terminal, share a live audience view over a URL, run code from your slides, collect survey responses, and it all stays on your machine.

## The home menu

SLOTH opens on a home screen. Move with the arrow keys (or `j`/`k`) and choose with Enter. Every screen uses the same arrow + Enter pattern.

| Item | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| New | Start a new deck from a template (Blank, Code-ready, Talk) |
| Open | Pick a deck and view/present it in the terminal |
| Present | Pick a deck and present it to an audience on a shareable URL |
| Edit | Pick a deck and edit it in the built-in editor |
| Survey | Build an audience survey with a guided wizard and add it to a deck |
| Upload | Add a .md file (or a project) from your computer |
| Download | Save the current project, including survey results, as a zip |
| Files | Browse the project folders; create and edit supporting files |
| Controls | View and rebind the keys used while presenting |
| Settings | Pick a colour theme (built-in or Gogh) and fine-tune its colours |
| Tutorial | Open the bundled intro deck |

* * *

## Writing a deck

A deck is a markdown file. Slides are separated by a line that is just `---` (three or more `-`, `*`, or `=`).

```markdown
%title: My talk
%author: Your Name
%date: 2026-06-20

# Title slide

A subtitle.

---

## Second slide

- a point
- another point
```

Deck features:

-   **Metadata**: `%title`, `%author`, `%date` lines at the top show in the bars.
-   **Big headers**: a top-level `# Heading` renders as large ASCII art.
-   **Centering**: `-> centered text <-` centers a line.
-   **Step reveal**: a line that is just `<br>` (or a lone `^`) is a pause; the slide reveals up to each pause as you advance.
-   **Speaker notes**: `<!-- speaker_note: only you see this -->`.
-   **Wikilinks**: `[[other-deck]]` links between decks; follow with `]`, go back with `[`, and see the link map with `m`.
-   **Code highlighting** for fenced code blocks.

### Directives

Directives are HTML comments. They never appear on a slide; they drive an action or pull in a file. Bare filenames resolve against the matching project folder (so `media/photo.png` and `photo.png` both find `media/photo.png`).

| Directive | Effect |
| --- | --- |
|  | Image (real image on the audience portal; the terminal can't show images) |
|  | Render that file's live contents on the slide |
|  | A download link on the audience portal |
|  | Run a snippet (default node ; add a command to override) |
|  | Run a full app on a shareable portal |
|  | Write the next code block to a file and run it |
|  | An audience form (see Surveys) |

Press `r` while presenting to run a slide's snippet/repo/code; output streams in a run pane. If it opens a port, the portal URL opens beside the terminal.

* * *

## Presenting

**Open** views a deck in your terminal. **Present** also serves a live audience page on a shareable BrowserPod portal URL: you drive from a presenter view (current slide, speaker notes, next-slide peek) and the audience page follows your slide changes over SSE. `s` toggles the audience render between clean HTML slides and a terminal look.

Default presenting keys (rebind any of them under **Controls**):

| Key | Action | Key | Action |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| → / l / space | next slide / reveal | t | table of contents |
| ← / h | previous | / | search the deck |
| g / G | first / last slide | o | open another deck |
| - then digits | jump to slide number | e | edit this deck |
| r | run this slide's code | m | link map |
| R | reload deck from disk | n | toggle speaker notes |
| ] / [ | follow wikilink / back | T / c | cycle / configure theme |
| q / Esc | back to the menu |  |  |

Arrow keys, space, Home/End, and Enter always work regardless of custom bindings, so navigation can never be stranded.

* * *

## Surveys

Surveys collect audience responses during a Present session. Build one with the **Survey** wizard (arrow + Enter): set an id, add questions, and write it into a deck. Each question is one of:

-   **single** — pick one (radio buttons)
-   **multi** — pick many (checkboxes)
-   **rating** — 1 to 5
-   **text** — free text

The wizard writes a directive like:

```markdown
<!-- survey id: feedback
single: How was this talk? | Great | Good | Could be better
multi: Which parts landed? | Intro | Demo | Q&A
rating: Rate the pacing
text: Any other comments?
-->
```

On the audience portal this renders as a form. Each submission is appended to `data/responses-<id>.csv` (with a header row), saved in the Pod filesystem. **Responses never leave the browser.** Get them out via **Download**, which includes the `data/` folder in the project zip.

* * *

## Projects

You can author a whole talk as a folder outside SLOTH (in Obsidian or any editor), then upload it. SLOTH reads this convention:

```
my-project/
  content/        .md decks
  media/          images, video, audio
  attachments/    downloadable files
  snippets/       inline runnable code
  repositories/   full apps to run on portals
  config/         project settings (below)
  data/           survey responses (CSV), written locally
```

-   **Download** exports the active project in this structure (wrapping a loose deck into `content/` if needed), including any survey results in `data/`.
-   **Upload** brings a `.md` file or project back in.
-   **Files** browses these folders inside SLOTH to create and edit supporting files.

### config/sloth.json

Optional per-project defaults, layered under the menu's Settings and Controls:

```json
{ "theme": "dracula", "notes": true, "highlight": true }
```

`theme` can be any built-in or Gogh theme name. The project's `config/theme.json` and `config/controls.json` override the global ones if present.

* * *

## Themes

Settings opens a searchable, scrollable theme picker. SLOTH ships its own built-ins plus the full [Gogh](https://github.com/Gogh-Co/Gogh) theme set (hundreds of palettes), rendered with 24-bit truecolor. Type to filter, Enter to apply (saved to `theme.json`), Tab to fine-tune individual colours, including custom `#rrggbb` hex values.

* * *

## How it stays client-side

SLOTH runs as a Node process _inside_ the Pod, so it reads and writes files, spawns commands, and serves the audience page all within the browser sandbox. The page and the Pod coordinate through the shared Pod filesystem and a few `xdg-open` sentinels (which BrowserPod surfaces to the page as `onOpen` / `onPortal` events) for things only the browser can do: opening a file dialog, downloading a zip, showing a portal. No survey response, deck, or file is sent to any server.

* * *

## Layout

```
sloth/
  sloth.js              the SLOTH app (parser, renderer, all the TUI modes)
  lib/
    themes.js           theme roles + truecolor painting, built-ins + Gogh
    gogh-themes.js      generated Gogh palettes (built by scripts/)
    controls.js         configurable present-mode key bindings
    editor.js           the modeless text editor
    slide-html.js       markdown -> HTML for the audience portal
    present-server.js   the in-Pod audience server (SSE + survey CSV)
    big-headers.js      ASCII-art big headers
    graph.js            ASCII wikilink map
    wrap.js, project.js word-wrap; project-convention resolution
  src/main.js           the page: boots the Pod, ships SLOTH in, runs it
  scripts/              build-pod-modules, build-gogh-themes
  decks/                bundled demo decks (intro, features)
```
